speaker: Sam Schrager
words: This conversation with Oscar Olson and Hazel Hill Olson took place at their home near Deary on June 16th, 1976. The interviewer was Sam Schrager.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about him?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I remember him running a hay baler one time. I don't know outside of that. I don't remember a great lot about him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember Chuck and Roy?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I remember Chuck and Roy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they like?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Roy used to be a sheep herder and shearer. He used to shear sheep. He'd come down here and did that one time. Good at it too. I understood that he liked the sheep work better than he did the woods work. I don't ever remember him working in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But Chuck did all the time, didn't he? Didn't Chuck do that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes, I think Chuck did work quite a bit in the woods. But then he owned that little place up there. He had 40 acres up there, the deer that he lived on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the folks' place? His parents' place? Or was that another one?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, it was a different one. His parents' place is the one that Buswell has now.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Isn't that right?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, it seems to me like when I first came... Tom Wyomberg. Oh, well, and that would have been before I came here. Because Tom and I lived there when I first came here.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh. And then Mary, the sister, she lived there in... Where Bert... Where Bert's son... Bert's son's from. She had a log cabin there.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Now that log cabin had some other history too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What other history did that log cabin have?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't know. It was a school or...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I thought it was the old school. Well, Mary's cabin was the school. Mary's was an old school. I was going to say there was some other history to that. Yeah, that was the Wells School. And after they built the Dairy School, this is what I've heard, then she took the... They moved it over and she lived in it then. That was the old school. That's very possible. Oh, it had been moved.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Is that it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: To the site that she lived in. Because it had been the local school. I remembered some kind of history. It set up on the other road, I think, that I couldn't quite remember the whole story about. But I'm curious about how much they were accepted and, you know, and what they thought. Oh, just fine. Just fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oscar went to school with some of the kids, didn't you?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes. I went to school with Mary's kids and they were just accepted the same as anybody else.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And Oscar said, just as sharp as could be. You bet. Just as sharp as could be.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I believe that Louisa was the best hand writer I ever saw. She had a beautiful hand writer.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: The... I understand that Joe, at least a lot of people say that Joe drank a lot, especially in his later years. Well, I think back in the old saloon days, nearly everyone around here drank. It used to be a pretty tough community in the early days. She wasn't a good scrapper and a good drinking man. You just didn't fit in too good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it that way by the time you were grown or still there?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh. Mostly when you were just a kid. It had to have been when I was a kid or even before that, I guess. Well, would you say it was mostly...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: was it mostly a lumberjack town in them days?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I think so, yeah. Woods working, putting the railroad in, and then of course they had to work on the roads every year for one or three days or something like that to pay their road tax.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Every man had to do that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Everybody, I think so. Do you remember fighting in the town when you were a kid, seeing a...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, it was... I should think you should tell about your dad. About your dad. You've told me several different things about your dad, having to get in fights.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, I don't know. My dad was the left kind. There couldn't be a good argument unless he had to be in the middle of it.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: They had quite a time with the old... Well, it must have been... well, Joe Wells down here at the saloon. That's when the saloon was down here. It seemed like Chuck and...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I believe it was Chuck and Louis Swenson got in a fight, and Louis Swenson could handle Chuck, all right. And this was the old Louis. This was the... The old Louis that lived up on Spud Hill there. And then the old man, old Joe come and he got into the fight, and he got Louis down and he was going to get his jackknife out and cut his throat. My dad picked up a piece of 2x4 and told him if he didn't put that knife away, leave that knife in his pocket, he was going to take his head right off, you know, with a 2x4. So he left. The fight was over. They had a trial over that too. But, you know, they didn't want to send old Joe Wells to the penitentiary either, see. So they didn't convict him. They didn't put press charges. Oh, but he never did. He didn't really do nothing. I mean, he didn't use the knife or nothing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he? What?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He didn't really use the knife. No, no, no, no. He didn't get that far. He was digging his pocket for the knife, but when he seen my dad threatening him of that too before.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, then why did they even have a trial, I wondered?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Getting him for battery or something?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, so what else did your father scrap or get into? I mean, with that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I don't think he ever avoided one. Just never avoided a fight of any kind. I don't think he really stirred up trouble or anything like that, but there was anything to be had around him, any trouble he had to be in, it seemed like.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you give me an idea about what the kind of stuff would be that would cause an altercation or, you know, a fight in those days?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Nothing in particular, just drinking and get rowdy and...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Drinking?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, drinking would be the thing, I think.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I think you'd be living in close quarters, you know, I mean, all in the same community and kind of isolated. You maybe get on each other's nerves through the years, you know. Well, I just kind of think so, yeah. I think it's pretty much that way.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But I think they were great for sticking together too. I really do. And helping one another out.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Tell him the one instance when they really stuck together and wouldn't buy this place from your dad.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh. Hey, my dad was married first to Arthur Burke's sister, and right over on the hillside there, her clothes caught on fire and she burned. Burned to death. And she died the next day, I remember. And I guess my dad felt pretty bad, you know, about that. And he was going to sell this place. He was going to sell it and get out because he'd always been just a lone wolf and traveler because he left home when he was 13 years old and never seen his folks again, see, and so he just depended on himself. He was going to sell the place and leave. And this preacher that was preaching at that time, he right in front of the whole congregation, he says, now, Oldson wants to sell his place and leave, and he says, we won't buy the place and we won't let anybody else buy it. We're going to keep them. You know, he knew the time would heal it after a while, I guess. So he did. Stayed. He had to stay. I wonder how he took it at the time. He was a little mad that they wouldn't let him go or not. Well, I don't know. I think he had a lot of respect for the preacher.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: What was the preacher's name?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Andreasen. Oh, I've heard of him. Andreasen. He was a Lutheran preacher. He was over in Genesee too, I think. I think he had to come, you know, maybe, I think he was in more than one of the churches. Oh, I think so. Probably, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was that church at?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: It stood right down here on this place, down here on the cemetery.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On the dry, where the Dry Ridge Cemetery is now?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, no, no, right down here between here and the highway. Oh, the cemetery.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And the big Lutheran church that's in Deery now, was down there. Oh, I'm moved. They moved.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How was it that his wife's clothes caught on fire?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, they were clearing land, clearing land, you know. And remember how they had four, five, or six petticoats, and they'd all drug on the ground, and I suppose you walked through where there was hot ashes or something. And the clothes in them days were pretty inflammable, you know. They caught a fire, and she burned.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And old Arthur Burke told Oscar just not too long ago that she was also pregnant at that time. Yeah. That she was. That she was.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And the time. Well, they'd been married a year. At that time, this happened in 1896. Well, you see, at that time, there was no doctor locally. And I think it was my dad headed to ride clear to Joel before they could even call a doctor in Moscow for a doctor to come out. But there was nothing they could do. But that's tough. That can go through the days like that. It'd be tough any time. But you know, in a way, it seems like a person should never have to be dressed like that to be doing that kind of work. But you know, that was the fashion in those days.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You wore those petticoats even if you were out clearing land. Yeah.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: We think it would be awful silly now, wouldn't we?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That happened right across the draw here. It was in that hillside there.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And for many, many years, we had a picture of this. Yeah, of her. It was her wedding picture. Your dad's wedding picture. And then when Arthur Burke and his sister were here, what we decided it was about 10 years ago, we showed it to them. And it really didn't have any sentimental value. Not as much for us as it would have for Arthur and his sister. And his sister is 100 years old this year. And it was about 10 years ago. I guess it wasn't quite 10 years ago. And we asked if she would like to have it. She just looked at it so longingly. It was a beautiful big, there would be a wedding picture and she was a beautiful girl. And of course she would like to have it. And so we gave it to her. We gave that to her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Your father, did he come out here when he left home? Or did he travel around?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He was a sailor. He was a sailor. He went across the Atlantic seven times. That old sailboat. Because he was born in Sweden. He was born in Sweden. And then when he come to this country, he, well, I really don't know about what just happened then. But I think he jumped ship when he come to this coast. And then he changed his name and he'd come over to South Dakota. And he worked there a couple of years. And then he'd come from there out here. He worked in these sawmills, you know, like you have pictures of there. And then he, oh, he homesteaded. And then he lost that claim somehow. He couldn't find his claim the second year. So he lost that. But then he bought this. He bought this place here from old John Thompson. You say he couldn't find his claim.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean he actually couldn't locate it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Couldn't locate it the second year. He had a place there. Wonder around where it was at. Well, it was east of Elk River somewhere. I can understand that. But I remember him saying that he, when they stayed at night there, the one they went there to prove up on the first time, why he said he could hear the river running down below him somewhere. But when he went back the second year to continue proving up on it, well, he couldn't find it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So about what year did he get this place? This place here?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah. Well, it had to be about 1890, I think. Early. About 1890, I think. Because he came to the United States in 88. And it was, she worked in Dakota then for two years. And then he come out here. But he set about to get himself a claim pretty quickly after he'd been here. I think so, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think he missed the sailing life?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You think he missed the sailing life going into the... Oh, I don't think so. No, I don't, no. I never heard him mention that.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: We have his old sea chest.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, yes. We got the old sea chest. That's great. But he had...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: It's about the only thing we do have that's that old. Yeah.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And the kitchen...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Did you ever see one of those old sea chests?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: We'll have to show it to you.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Should we just go upstairs?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah. It's simpler than bringing...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Is that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I am not... Probably. Well, probably. Because it wasn't long after he married her that, you know, it wouldn't be too many years before you were born. And that was the bedroom you were born in. But I do remember your mother saying that she helped him. Now, maybe it was just finish up the upstairs. But I know she said that she helped your dad do some of this carpentry work. So maybe it was just finishing up the upstairs.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That's possible. Did he clear up this land or was it... Oh, yes. You bet. You bet. There wasn't anything here when he could... Oh, he often spoke about just the big yellow pine stand around here. And they could get out to the side and cut a little grass in between the trees and have some for the cattle for a cow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of work did you do around here when you were a boy?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, growing land, clearing that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is that it? You cleared too?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You bet. That was a big job to clear this.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You especially remember when World War I was over, you said.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: What you were doing back then. Yes, sir. You bet. When World War I was plowing up here, my dad was shooting stumps. And I was trying to plow. I was only about 12 years old. But I could hang on to that plow. And as it happened, he had eight or ten stumps loaded about the time when we heard that the war was over. And it seemed like it was very appropriate to shoot ten stumps right then. Self -pressure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You shot him then?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go into Deary? Huh? Did you go into Deary at that time to celebrate too? The end of the war?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I think so, yeah. Yeah, we had to go in there and listen to the celebration.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And then it might be of interest about the bad flu and how you escaped the bad flu. Oh, by golly, yes. That 1919, 1918 -19 flu. That was terrible. I've heard of that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: My goodness. There were so many people died around here. It was terrific.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now did they close down the school?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Because you said you stayed right here at the farm. Yeah, yeah, we did. We stayed right here. And I remember that fall we was clearing the land right over on the, right over there by the woods.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That was a bad deal. We escaped the Tar River. We didn't get sick at all. But our neighbor over here was sick. And we delivered milk over to him. And I didn't dare to go into the house. They wouldn't let me do that. I'd take the milk over and set it in the road by their place. And one of them wasn't too sick, went out and got it. Oh, it was quite a scare.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that your mother and the work that she did was pretty difficult to do without the modern conveniences that people have nowadays?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: So I'll say so. I'll say so. Pack water from the spring down there up the hill here.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Everything done by hand. And your mother worked right out in the field, too. Oh, my mother worked out a lot.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: It took real good people.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Now she lived to be 88. 89.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: 89 was it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: 89, I believe. 89, I believe. And I think it was true that she'd never been to a doctor until she broke her arm when she was 85 or so. I'm not positive, but I believe that's what she said.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Never been to a doctor until she broke her leg. Her arm.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And that was when she was old.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When you were growing up, did you see doctor at all?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Or was it... No way.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No. No, we didn't have no doctors. Never took no shots for nothing.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: When did you first see a doctor, do you suppose? In your life?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That's a good question. I'd be damned if I know.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Probably when you had your tonsils real bad. When you were in camp. Well, that was after I was working in logging camp. That's what I say. Probably that's the first time you ever went to a doctor. I think so.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Listen, did you go to Deary much when you were young or growing up?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: How much did you... Oh, we went to school. Went to school. You went there in town. Oh, yeah. Walked from your house here. It's only a mile and a half. It was just good exercise.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You might tell me what you remember about the fire, the Deary fire.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I just remembered being a fire. I don't know if I remember a great lot more about it than that. I thought you said that there were great, even great big sheets of things that would come clear down here. Oh, yeah. There was pieces of shingles. So it was clear down here.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: What year was that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I think it was 23. At least that's the year that I've heard. That's possible. Now there was more than one, but the worst one was 23. Oh, yes. There was several fires in Deary.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: 23 is out. I believe that's about right. But I remember those, like, shingles laying all over the country.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you start working in the woods when you came of age to work out? Is that what you done?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Sure was.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He was a bad boy and quit high school to go work in the woods.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I quit high school to go work in the woods.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How come?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I knew more than the teacher, so I didn't miss seeing objects in the whole school. All she asked me was all those nouns and verbs and adverbs, and I didn't care about that.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Didn't you say you got mad at the teacher? Did you get, what was it over that you got mad?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I thought she was being partial. I didn't like that very good. So he hurt her by quitting school. Oh, wow.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Yeah, you being partial to certain kids?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes. Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that at? Was that, were you in high school at the time?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So then you went to work in the woods?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Then I went to work on the section. Went down to Princeton and went to work in the section, on the section. Camp One, Princeton. From then on, it was work, work, work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How do you wish you to stay in school?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes, I wished I hadn't. My dad just, he just tried everything in the book to get me to go back to school, but I was just too stubborn.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: It would be interesting too, where you went to school, where the high school was.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Where the high school was?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, yeah. The high school was in the church house up on the, on the west side of the crack. Frank Campbell's shop. Made into a shop. That's where I went to high school. When did you... Start with, and then, and then they transferred over into the other building.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you didn't, did you?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I went there a little while.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Did you? Into the, into the new? What is the brick high school now?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes. Oh, I didn't realize that you had ever gone there. When, when did you... Half a year, I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When did you regret that you would, that you had left school?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: How long did it take you to...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, how long did it take to realize that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, it didn't take too many years, I guess. I think I realized that before I... You told me that you wanted so to go back, but you had, did you were just too stubborn. Yes. You told me that many times. I went to get on the train to go down to go to work there.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And I just knew I was making a mistake. I just felt terrible. But the decision had been made and boy, I was going to stick with it. Stubborn is the word.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like in the, living in the camps?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, that was good. That was, that was fine.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That was camp one down there. It was pretty modern camp. Railroad cars.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Which year would that have been then? You were born in six and when did you start working for it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, let's see. I don't know just what year that was. You were probably about 16. Yes, you bet. It's quite a, quite a deal in 1919. You know, the IWWs, the Frick was pretty vicious. And then the IWWs, they got blamed for setting a lot of fires out in the harvest fields, you know, planting matches in the shocks and stuff like that. I don't know whether they did that or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when they were the strongest would be for, when you were still in school, was before you went? Well, do you think they were responsible for improving the conditions in the camps?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, they improved the conditions. You bet they did. Before that, the loggers were carrying their own blankets and stuff like that. Oh, they helped to beat everything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many, do you remember when you went to the camp were there many guys that were still Wobblies working in the camps at that time?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, I think there were always a certain amount of them. And then the strike in 36, you know, that was, but I don't think they gained anything then. I don't think they had anything to squawk about at that time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened in that 36 strike?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, it was just a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What were they striking for? Better wages?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Better conditions, wages, I guess.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they shut down the camps in the woods around here?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, I see them. But I don't think they gained anything at that time. They just lost cause from the start, I think.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: One fellow that I know who's a... went out of there, those gunmen on there. And there was no hired gunman either. It was just lumberjack. It was just fellow who had been working there all the time. When these two fellows come into camp that evening and they come through this picket line and they beat them up with chains. You could, this one fellow, you could just count the links of the chain right across his face. And the end had hit his ear and damn near tore his ear off. And they told him to go back to camp and tell the rest of the fellows what they had coming. They were going to get the same treatment. And these guys said, oh, if that's the case, we'll go out and meet them halfway. So they were the one that had a gun and grabbed the gun and jumped in this pickup. And they went out to meet these fellows. And when they met up, they started shooting. Two or three of them got shot, but I don't think either one of them died from that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this Rainier Pierce? This was a lot of Pierce, see that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: From Elts Camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So the guys that beat him with the chain, they were IWWs?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: The ones that did the beating. Well, I don't know if it was IWWs, but they were on the picket line. They were the strikers. They were the strikers, yeah. The picket line and these other guys would come through the picket line.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where were you at in this?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I was in the camp. I was right there in camp.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I was scaling, see. I couldn't belong to the Union, and I was in that camp.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But were you considered on the management side?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes. The reason you couldn't come up with a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Because you were a scaler?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And it's a good thing I didn't have a gun, because I think I would have been on that truck with them. And I'm glad I didn't. Very glad I didn't. But as I remember, one fellow got shot through the shoulder, and I think six months later, I'd seen him in Orofino and he had his arm way up over his head like that.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't think any of them died from that, did they?

speaker: Sam Schrager or Oscar Olson
words: Well, that's what Michigan Bill said, but maybe it was even another incident. Maybe it was another thing that happened in the same area. Well, this was the only time I think... The only time you knew it was shooting. It was quite a shooting scrape, and they called the militia. The state militia. Yeah, that's the only time. Oh, I remember. Do you remember those guys coming into the camp after... Is that... Did you see them get beat, or did you see them... No, I didn't see them get beat. I'd seen them after they come into camp, and they were beat up, I tell you. You beat a man up with a butt chain, and he's in bad shape. And you could count the links across his face, that butt chain. I tell you, when you see that, you begin to wonder if it was in some way to stop that sort of thing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the feeling in the camp was all of one side? I mean, do you think that in the camp all of them wanted to put a stop to it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, you're damn right. Those fellows in camp, they were determined, and no more of that stuff.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Now, all of the ones that were in camp were the ones that were breaking the strike.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Is that it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Or were men... Well, like me and others.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how come it was so divided? How come the men didn't agree on either one side or the other?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: How come they were... Well, I couldn't tell you that. I don't know. I don't know. I think quite a lot of the guys felt like they had no striking coming or anything like that.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, it's funny to talk to someone that knew anything about it. Oh, I've heard a lot about that 18 strike, not too much about the 36 one. Although Charlie Jelliberg talked to me about that 36 strike. I suppose Charlie would remember that real good. Yeah, he told me of a guy that got beat on that side that came up over here. And he saw when one day he happened to have his... I don't know if he had his shirt off or something, but he just saw the... You know, the wounds on the guy's back. And they asked him. He said he felt real funny about even asking a guy about it. You know, how it had ever happened. But that's... That was it. Same deal. Been violence over there. Well, those things always have their humorous sides too. I know one guy that was a great agitator. He had an awful gab. Boy, you get up in the woodsheds and he was agitating that we were going to have a strike and they were really going to raise hell. They were going to bring the machine guns in and they were just going to tear things to pieces. And the day before the strike was called, why, he got a hell of a toothache. Got an awful toothache. He had to go to town and he never showed up as long as that strike was in effect. But you know, those kind of fellows can stir up a mob real easy. And he did his share. But boy, when it got to where the going might get rough. He had a toothache.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was this the same strike or the other one?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: This was the 36th.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Now you were telling Claire and Bud about...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Didn't they appoint you as a militia?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: This was the same night when those guys went out and did the shooting. A little later that evening, the policeman come in there and he deputized me and another fellow to go out and guard that road in the camp that night before the militia could get there.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: It sounds like a good bit of a dangerous assignment. Yeah, you bet. Took good cool nerves and I didn't have too many of them. But anyway, told me to get down below the road there behind a big log and there was another about space like that and there was another log over there so I had real good protection there. Good view. And the other fellow got up on the bank above the road and this policeman, he stopped in the road. He says, now he says we're going to stop everybody that comes in here. And he says they've got to stop. So we sit there. Boy, I was kind of nerved up. I didn't like that too good. This little cop sat there. After a while here we heard the rig coming. And boy, I tell you, it was tense there for a little bit. Luckily it turned out it was the militia coming. Of course they stopped too, believe you me they did. When we found out who it was, well then we could go back to camp and he picked up these six shooters.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: We was off duty.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Did you say that was Laughing Jim Delaney?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah. That was the cop, that was the policeman that deputized you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Laughing Jim? Where'd he get that name from, you know?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He laughed a lot. No doubt. If you ever heard him laugh, you'd never wonder why.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I didn't know him too. Hazel knew him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of laugh did he have? What was the deal?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well it started out as kind of a little chuckle and then developed more and more and it became kind of hysterical and then everybody around him had a laugh. That's fact.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he laugh easy?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh yes. Well he knew he had a failing there and he kind of tried to hold back on it but once he got started, it just couldn't hardly stop him. Sounds like Jimmy Carter's smile. Yeah. Jim was a good guy though. He was a real fine fellow.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Does Jim still live?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I think so. I think he's still living.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, now did you have, like these fellows that shot these guys on the truck, were they the same guys that got beat, the ones that did the shooting? Or were they other guys?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, the two guys that got beat. They were ready to lay down when they got to camp, I tell you. They were hurting.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: They were beat up. Friends of the ones that got beaten up.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Those were the fellows that had been working together all the time, you know, friends.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now what about these guys on the picket line? Were some of them from the camp too? Were the guys who'd been working in the camp before?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I imagine. I didn't know who they were but I imagine so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what kind of picket line they had? Was it right? Were they just holding the road so people couldn't get in?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Stopping the road. And Foreman wouldn't try to, Foreman didn't try to do nothing about it. Oh, no way. When they got these guys though, were they, were the guys that they shot, were they in the back of the truck at the time they, that they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: When they did the shooting?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I think so. I'm not positive. They weren't just standing on the road. Well, I don't know. What I heard that when the first shot was fired, why they just took off into the brush, you know. It was in the woods and luckily somebody didn't get killed on the spot. These guys that did the shooting, nothing ever happened to them, I imagine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean they didn't get, no charges were pressed against them or what was it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: The company had something to do with that. I don't know, you know, they were kind of on the company's side and I don't know just how they were protected but I guess there was no shortage of funds for protecting them.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: It's funny then, what were you going to say?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I was going to say, pretty exciting days, Altonia. I'm thinking about how different it was between then and 1917 or 18 there. That first IWW business, I had the idea that most everybody in the woods, at least all the working men, were on their side because they wanted the conditions improved because they were bad. This in 1936 sounds like a completely different deal. Well, I think so, yeah. I wonder why it changed so much. Maybe because the conditions were pretty good by then. Well, yeah, they were not bad. They were not bad. I didn't think they were bad.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You had clean sheets that they provided clean every week?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Lots of food, good food.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And I couldn't see but what conditions was real reasonable. A lot of those fellows that were raising all the hell would go to town and live on beans, maybe all winter, down by the railroad tracks somewhere. Now there's camp, they're kicking on the conditions there. It didn't make sense.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Now I said clean sheets, they weren't. They were blankets, but they brought them. But they... Blankets. We had sheets too.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Did you?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You bet. I just remembered...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Blankets. Sheet blankets. Yeah, sheet blankets. Sheet blankets is what I mean. Instead of, you know, not percale.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Most of these guys, most of these guys though that were in the idea, I mean, in 1936, would you say that they were mostly local men, I mean lumberjacks who were just discontented? Or were they more, or do you think they were mostly people who'd come in from, you know, who were sent in just for the striking?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I mean hard for me to believe there could be, that they'd have that many people to even send in if there wasn't some local, some local support, but I... It was mostly, well of course the camps even in 36 was a lot of transients. There's a lot of transient labor, but I don't think there was anyone really sent in to stir up a lot of trouble. I don't think so. It was a few of these fellows like the guy I was telling you about that got the toothache.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But they didn't even call them IWWs in 1936, did they?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I don't know.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Didn't they call them?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I just really don't know, but from reading history you heard about the IWWs so much back in 1919.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I don't think they had the clout in 36, right?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And then in 36 you just heard of them as strikers, you know, you didn't hear of them as Wobblies, because they got a pretty bad name for a while there. What do you mean back in 19... Back in 1919, you know, a lot of people didn't want to feel that they were affiliated with them, and even though they might have thought the same in 36, I'm just saying what I have read, I don't know anything about it. I think that's about right.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, let me ask you a little bit about the lumberjack life. Now, I understand these guys were mostly bachelors, and they mostly would work in a camp for a long time, then they'd blow in. Yeah, that's about right. That's what I've heard. Yes, I'd say that is true. Some would work on the coast wild, and some back in Michigan, and some other place, and go from one place to another.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And every month or so they'd go out and spend all their money, didn't they? How long would they work before they'd blow in?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, some had to blow in every payday, and some would go maybe all summer before they'd blow in.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: When you were there, it was twice a month that they paid, wasn't it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, you had to draw day. You could draw in the middle of the month. Otherwise, it was once a month. Once a month.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oscar was camp boss at 14. Oh. Camp 14. Ended up at that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was 14 at?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: On Beaver Creek.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: We went back last week. That's where I met her. Last week to Camp 14, just to see what it looked like back there. I was back, I was there, well, it was 42, the summer of 42, fluking.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That's where she and I got it from. And that's where I met Oscar when he was sitting there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the fluming being used there when you were on the Beaver Creek Fluming?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: The fluming was, yes, definitely being used at one time while I was over there.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't remember whether it was used the last year or there or not. The signs last week said that it was used. 41. The last that it was used was 42, it said. 42. And I remember that, I don't ever see that, do you ever remember seeing them being used, but we used to walk on them and they were in real good condition, whereas now they're all down. You can see a little part of them now. The sign said they were only used for 12 years from 30 to 42.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it like, were you formed there when the thing was being used?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, I was, no. Well, it never flumed out of 14 anyway. That was below 14. Oh, I see. Logs out of Camp, oh, went down into Doyle Dam. From there on down. 14 was a railroad camp, see, they shipped the logs out on the railroad. It was a beautiful camp, it was just a show place.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Old George McKinnon, when I first went in there, he was the camp boss, and his wife lived in there with him, and she was a great flower fancier, a great gardener, and she had lots of help from the, what would it be, the bull cooks?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Bull cooks, and anyone else would help her, you know. She had, they had flower gardens all over that camp, and it was, it just looked like a park instead of a logging camp. I couldn't believe that that was a logging camp when I went in there. Beautiful lawn. Because it was just so pretty. All watered and cared for, it was really a show place.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But boy, you'd never know it now. No, you can't. You can't recognize the spot.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That's unusual, I've never heard of a logging camp that was set up to be pretty. Well, it sure was. This one was, and I'm sure it was because of Mrs. McKinnon. Yes, Mrs. McKinnon. And then later in 42, then I went back to teaching, I was teaching at Kellogg that year, and then, what must have been the long, what, maybe September, no, I guess maybe August, that George McKinnon had a heart attack. Yes, August. And then Oscar. I called her over then. As the camp boss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You were working sometimes as a flunky and sometimes as a school teacher?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I was teaching. Yes, she was a school teacher. And then I was teaching in his purse that year.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And I can't remember how we heard, whether we read it in the paper, can't remember, but there was, well, the men were, all the young men that had been flunkies were drafted and that they were wanting girls to come in. And so I was one of the first girls that went in to Camp 14. They had had women flunkies at other camps on this side, but I was one of the first ones that went in to Camp 14. That was quite a heyday. That was a great day for celebration. And the only way you could get in there at that time was by train. Train or speeder.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: How many miles from headquarters was it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, 16 miles. Oh, I don't know. I thought it was 20 something. It just seemed forever.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And I thought, oh my goodness, what am I getting myself in?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I'm getting myself in a And then we got, when we got back, there was this beautiful camp back in there. As I said, just almost like a park. Yeah, pretty flowers are grown all the way around. And then, as luck would have it, a girlfriend of mine came in just a little bit later than that. And we didn't come together. No, she came later. She came later, but I had gone to school with her. And so I knew her and we had a great time that summer. And went back to teach school. And I taught until November and came back and married Oscar. And then we lived in there. Then we called it our honeymoon cottage. And we honeymooned at camp 14. That was our first residence. And we couldn't even see a sign. We just could hardly tell where our honeymoon cottage was last weekend.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Because it was just When you, uh, uh, flunky, what was that, what was the work of funking like? Was it a hard thing for you to adjust to? Was it a lot of work?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I heard it was fairly heavy work. It was hard work, but I was young, you know. And it was an adventure.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: So, and we'd get up early. We had to get up real early and we worked late. And she had a great appetite and we had a lot of good food. And I remember old Bill Burke saying, Now you girls shouldn't stay up late. You shouldn't do this because you're going to get too tired. And they're going to say, I'm overworking you.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Remember old Bill?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, you bet. But, uh, oh, it was such a, so much different than anything we'd ever done. It was so much fun to get out and walk on those hills. You know, we'd go up to the top of them and just, and I remember the cook's wife was in there occasionally.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And she'd say, what do you young people have? Do you have, are you taking some kind of a vitamin X that you can run to the top of those hills every night and work so hard all day?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: It was hard work, but it was fun. It was really fun. An Oscar technique issue.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Kind of like going through those days again. Had never worked as a weight restore anything before that. Didn't have any idea what I was getting in for.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Can you tell me what the, what the main part of the work was for you to do? Did you have help?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Pairing the vegetables. Yes, we paired the vegetables. There was a dishwasher, a man that washed the dishes, but we put the dishes away, dried the silverware, Set the table. Set the tables. Carried the food on there. And that was the main job. To see that the hot cakes was good and hot. Oh, the lumberjack didn't want the hot cakes after they'd been off the path, off the cook stove for two minutes. But what I thought was the strangest of all was that that cook house was completely quiet. The, the, the lumberjacks didn't say a word except Pass the please. Pass the please. They didn't visit. Never. Not one word of visit. Cook house.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And that rule. You bet. You bet.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That's what amazed me the most. It wasn't like you're going to a cafeteria, you know. Tell me why they had that rule.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, because lumberjacks can say some of the damnedest things you ever heard of, you know. They can not only say dirty jokes, but they can talk bad about the food, and some fellows got weak stomachs, and there are many reasons for it.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And it was a good idea. And probably that they were anxious to get them in there and get them out, and that they were in there to eat, not to visit. They were in there to eat, and that was all. But to me, that was the strangest thing. All those great big men come in there, and they'd sit there just like little school children that were going to be spanked if they just, if they talked out of line. I was amazed. That was enforced by the camp boss and the cook too.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: The cook wouldn't stand for it. And it always been that way in the camps, Oscar was just. As long as I was in there, yeah.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: It was just as quiet, except for the rattling of dishes, and, and you would hear them say, pass the piece please, you know, pass the hot piece please.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But just. You bet. I think that was a good idea. If you just said, pass something, why, you may not get it. If you said please, you'd get it right away quick. And you didn't want to reach out in front of somebody to get something. You asked for it. If you reached in over somebody else's plate, he'd probably jab you with his fork or something. You just, you just didn't do that. And I thought that was good. That was fine. Real good. I approved of it. So there was, there was manners and courtesy. Damn right. There was manners and courtesy.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But it, like Hazel says, it did seem kind of strange to see a bunch of those big, tough, rugged burgers sitting in there behaving themselves.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, I'm sure that's what impressed me the most to begin with.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: They just were so quiet. Well, I heard the food was about the most important thing to a lumberjack. I mean, the food wasn't good. What use was the camp is the idea I've had. Oh yes, you bet.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: The food had to be good. I can only top for 14, but I've never seen such fantastic food in my life. I gained about 20 pounds. That's good. Too good, as far as I was concerned. I don't know how you could get better food.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And when they brought hotcakes out, they set a pan of hotcakes on there and they'd one time around and there's no use to pass that plate the second time around. Nobody'd take a cold hotcake. That went back to the cook's house and in the garbage pan and here come a new batch of hotcakes. All hot right off the griddle.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: If it ever got sat down, the pan of hotcakes, if it ever got sat down on the table, we were supposed to take it up and take it back to the kitchen and bring some hot ones because the lumberjacks wouldn't touch it if it didn't, you know, you would hand them and they'd pass it around.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Having pretty rough to keep, you know, how do you have enough stove space to keep them all hot?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You can't imagine how they cooked hotcakes. They had a grill as far from here to that wall. Almost like Paul Bunyan's. And a man of porn there all the time and another and coming right around behind him with a thing and turning them over and they just, he didn't get around until they were on the plates and out going out. And that was around the round till breakfast was over. You bet. You feed 140 men and hotcakes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That many in that camp? Did you find the food varied in the different camps you were in or did you find it always to be of this high quality?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I found it to be good. You bet. All of us. I think Bill Burke had an exceptional reputation as being a good cook. He was an exceptionally good one.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But I think that they were good cooks.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: They're living in Oregon. Right. We hear from him about once a year.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever work for the camp that Shorty Justice was cooking in?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes. I interviewed him up in Spokane about two summers ago. There was a good cook too. Shorty Justice.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And what was Thad's last name?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Thad was the second cook. They had a main cook and a second cook.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Thad somebody. But he was dead. Thad was a lot like I never heard. Thad's second.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that you received a lot of attention as being, I imagine it was rare for these lumberjacks to have any women in the camp at all? Was it 14?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I would think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were they awkward?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: We didn't see them. Seemed to be like they felt conscious of you. I think appreciative maybe, but I don't think they felt too uncomfortable. I don't ever remember. But they were awful good about leaving the showers at a certain time. Boy, they weren't. We were supposed to have the showers at a certain time each day. And they just, even the ones that were in camp, they just disappeared completely. They never were the least bit impolite in any way. They just cleared out. I don't know whether they'd been told to or they must have been told to. I don't think they had to do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many women were there, flunking at the time you were in here?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Four, I believe.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you meet right away when you came into the camp?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: It wasn't too long. Can't remember for sure. Wasn't very long. I liked your smile and I said I knew I'm going to get acquainted with that gal.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I can't remember. The first time I remember you is when I was ironing a blouse. Oh, you burned your blouse. Uh -huh. First time you noticed and you burned your blouse. I burned my blouse, uh -huh. She got excited and she left the iron on that too long. Oh, I don't know how that all came about. Then you asked one time if we wanted to ride out to, if Alabelle and I wanted to ride out to headquarters. It would have been. I had to go in and get something. You were going to take a speeder out. You were going to go get some fellows.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I was going to get some fellows and get the mail, wasn't it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I think so. I think so. So we decided that we would for something to do. And that's when old Me Too was so drunk.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes, that's right.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: What was Me Too's real name?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Ollie Vincent. Yeah, he was so drunk.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Anything you mentioned, he said Me Too. Me Too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that his nickname?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That was his, we nicknamed him after himself. I don't know whether anyone else called him Me Too or not. We called him Me Too. I don't even, didn't even know what his real name was after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was drinking like in the camps and around the camps?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No liquor was allowed in camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, does that mean there was no liquor in camp?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, no, it didn't mean that at all. There was liquor there, but it was held down pretty well. They would come back from town, liquored up pretty well, but they generally come back in a Sunday evening and by Monday morning they were generally ready to go to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have much of an idea about why it was this, I think I've been able to figure out about lumberjacks, is how come most of these guys could only save up their money to blow it and then come back broke?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I mean, what was this idea that you couldn't, you know, nowadays everybody thinks save, save, save, you know. Now these guys, it seems like they didn't have much, most of them didn't think about saving. Well, nearly all of them were single men, they had nothing to save for. They were concerned about tomorrow. I think it was the reason, I don't know. Now most of the lumberjacks are married men, got families or something.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: How many men was in camp though that were married men?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Not too many. Not too many.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: The ones that were on the rail were on the train. Yeah, the train men probably. But they didn't stay there either.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did they stay?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: It was their headquarters. At headquarters. So there were women living at headquarters there. They had a train men's shack that they would stay there right during the week, I think, or part of the time at them.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But I would say that the majority of the men back there were single in 1942, the ones at 14. Oh, I'm sure they were.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I'm trying to think now. I can't think of any of them that were married. Yeah, Johnny Sewell. Johnny Sewell was married. Well, and Walter Grew, he was married. Yes, but his wife was clear back. His wife was there in North Dakota.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I didn't know an awful lot of the men's names. I knew them by sight, you know. But there was so many of them I didn't even know who they, what their names were, whether they were married or not.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think about being the camp foreman after working in the camp? But did you find it, did you enjoy that responsibility, or did you find it a big headache?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, just another job, just part of the job. It didn't make much difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you were called straw boss before, which was kind of assistant, wasn't it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah. I worked up from working on the section, clear up the camp boss. So I did everything along the line. And it was just another advancement, a little more responsibility. It wasn't too much different than scaling or looking after a gang of saw, looking after the sawyers or looking after the cats or anything like that. It wasn't too much difference.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there any foreman that you had that you liked especially?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, yes. Yes, indeed. Old George McKinnon just was tops. He was the fellow. He had a heart attack there in August. And he went to the hospital and lingered along for a while and then died.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made him good?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was, I guess you'd have to call him an alcoholic, wouldn't you?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Once he had a...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What made him good?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: A good foreman.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: What made him a good foreman? A good foreman?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, he was just considerate of everyone. And I think he treated everyone alike. Oh, he was just a good guy. But he had a drinking problem. Yes, he did. Very bad.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And the sweetest wife?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, my, yes. She was just, I just loved her. She was just as sweet as she could be.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You know what would be interesting?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Telling some of the names, like Broomface Brooks. He's probably heard all those things. I never heard that one.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He was a camp foreman, wasn't he?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes, I worked for Broomface Brooks on Diamond. He always wore a mustache and it looked like a little broom sticking out there. So I thought one time while I'd ready a mustache too, and mine began to look just like his. I had to shave it off. I was afraid he'd fire me out of camp.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And then there was hard -hearted somebody.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Dangerous Dan.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Dangerous Dan?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the deal on him?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, he was the guy that, no, it was Cruel Jimmy. Cruel Jimmy Holmes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Holmes? Wasn't that his last name?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Might be, I don't know. All I've heard is the Cruel Jimmy part.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, do you know where he got that reputation?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oscar does, no. I think so.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He went on the drive. What I had heard. Oscar doesn't know for sure, but he'd heard this. I had heard that they were on the drive breaking a jam and his partner got his foot caught. The jam shifted a little bit and caught his foot and it was getting ready to leave, you know. And it was a cinch to take him down. A sudden death for his partner if he didn't free him. And he had an axe there right handy and he just chopped his partner's leg off right there. One blow of the axe cut his foot off.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Had you heard this before?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't know, but it sounds familiar enough that I may have. Now, Oscar has told me this for 35 years. I think I have heard a story very much like that. Cruel Jimmy after that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that the only thing he could have done to save his partner?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, there was no other way. He saved his partner by it, sure. Saved his life, but had to give up his life. Saved his life because that big jam was starting to boil and roll over, you know. Taking him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he strike you as a cruel person? Did you know him?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I didn't know him that well. I don't know. No, I don't. I don't. I never heard that about him. He was just a quick thinker and he had to do what had to be done. That's all. Broken ass John.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You remember that? Remember him?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He's heard of him too. I've heard that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Leo Guilfoy? What? Did you know Leo Guilfoy?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He's from Bovill. He worked in the Cedar yard. You might not have known him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Guilfoy?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, Guilfoy. Well, yeah, I remember Guilfoy, but... Leo. He told me that. He gave me that name. Broken ass...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's his last name?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: John.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was it? What was the matter with him?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I don't know. I believe he was a sawyer. But anyway, he'd put wedges in his pockets back here, you know, and he wouldn't have suspenders. They were strong enough to hold them up. And, you know, his pants had kind of sagged down and he looked kind of silly and hung that name on him, I think.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And then there was a Dirty Shirt Martin. Dirty Shirt Martin, you bet.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He was there in that camp. Knew him. He'd just buy a new shirt and he would never wash it. He'd let it war out and then he'd throw it away and get another shirt. And they called him Dirty Shirt Martin. I have pictures here of him, you know. Didn't you say that he came here and... Yes, he came here with me one time and helped put up hay over the Fourth of July one time.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And what were some of those other names?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Butterfly Pete. Butterfly Pete and Powderpuff McDonald. Now tell him why he was Butterfly Pete. Well, he seemed to be so light on his feet.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And he worked on the drive, you said. Yeah, he was around the river and...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Fluttered around the logs. Boy, he could flutter around a log, you bet. He could run out there on a little cedar pole that wouldn't hold up anybody and he'd have only oxfords on. He wouldn't get his feet wet. He'd just triple on them just as light as a feather.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And Powderpuff McDonald. Powderpuff McDonald.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Now where...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know where he got that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, he was a fellow that's an unusual guy. Generally, you know, lumberjacks go out there with horses and skid and tongs and this rigging, you know, dragging it around the mud and working the mud. They'd come mud clear up to their hips, you know. And he could go out and work with the team all day and he was nice and clean in the evening. He just wouldn't get no mud on him at all. I wonder how he did it. No, I don't know. He was using his head all the time, I guess. Well, some people at teams just have that talent to do things without getting... I can't work around anything like that. I have to get it all over my clothes the first thing, you know. But old Powderpuff, he was just cleaning the pen in the evening. I listened to Powderpuff McDonald one time. He even run camp once.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He was a camp boss?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He was a camp boss at one time. But I heard him. He was over there in, I believe it was 26 or something. It was one of those other camps, anyway. Oh, it was the camp that Broom Prince Brooks was running. And he was going to swamp for a couple Irishmen,

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Flanagan and Mike Tackney. They were two horsemen, you know. They were driving, the Teamsters. And they had a big strip there. And they got in the habit of, after a feller had swamped about so long, why they'd get miserable, you know, and holler at them and cranky and run this feller off. So then they would divide up the bonus on the strip. Well, they were going to try it on old Powderpuff McDonald, the same stunt. But I got old. I got old.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Was spouting off one day. They'd been snowing and it was kind of miserable. And oh, he said, we're stuck in line. We never get them logged out of there. And he was just having a hell of a time. Powderpuff, he says, oh, he says, we have horses and we've got cable and blocks and lines. And he says, we'll get them logged out. They couldn't run him off, no way. So he stayed and got his bonus off of that big strip.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Did he have a pleasant manner as well as keeping himself so spotless?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Powderpuff, he'd be very dead, you bet.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't think there's any way to get him mad or riled up. That sounds unusual for Lumberjacks. Well, I'd say that, yeah, kind of unusual.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Not for him. Good fall. I can't think of some of the other men. There are lots of them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever know Pack Sack Dick, Dick Farrell? Yes, yes, the preacher?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, yes, you bet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you run into him?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: In camp.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Up on this side or was he down there?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I think I ran into him the first time up in Elk River Basin about 1929.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But he also came through 14 that summer I was there. Yes, yes. He went all the camps. He went all over. That was in 42. So he was still going. He was still going. There was a preacher came through and I think it was Farrell. I think it was Farrell too. I do too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was he like, this Pack Sack Dick? Well, in what way would you mean?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I'm wondering, I don't know what his manner was. You have to be an old prize fighter.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I heard this about him. I don't know. I wasn't in the meeting at the time, but I heard that some lumberjack was trying to make some caustic remarks or something while he was having a sermon in the cookhouse. And on Dick stopped his sermon and he says, say, he says, I quit fighting years ago, but he says, don't think I can't start it up again. This is my meeting and I'm going to keep it at border here. That guy decided to take him to his word too, I heard.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That might be why he got along so well with these rough lumberjacks, is that they knew he could work them over physically. He was a scrapper too.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He was a nice fellow, real nice. You know, have any idea the kind of preaching that he did, the way he would talk to the men, whether he gave them, whether he told them they are damned or whether he told them they... No, I don't remember it being that way. No, no, I don't think so.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Did you go to some of his meetings?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes, I've been to his meetings.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: As I remember, he emphasized the fact that he used to be a rounder, the same as the rest of them, and that he had changed his ways and he thought that was for the better and stuff like that.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I admired him. He's a good fellow.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: How did he travel? Did he come in there on the train?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I can't... I think he just went from one camp to camp whenever there was a means to get by, to ride with somebody. I don't ever remember him driving a car or anything. The dedication though, he's there for so many years. I didn't realize he'd been in the camps for so long. That speaks something for his ideas. I believe that's right though. I think I've seen him up at Elk River the first time. I've seen him many, many years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know Malcolm Anderson?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes, yes, yes. I worked in the camp when Malcolm was boss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think of him as a boss?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Good man, good man. Awful heavy on the drinking.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: As were many of the Lumberjacks.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, very few Lumberjacks. But the Lumberjacks had a special trait though. My golly, 99 % of them you could trust them with anything. And they'd give you the shirt off their back if they thought it helped you. Of course, you always have that other 1 % that wasn't so good, but I guess you expect that everywhere. Well, what's surprising is the 99.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: What other line of work can you say that about them?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You could say that you could trust them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, like in what way? Loaning them money?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Anything, you bet, you bet. You could own money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You'd leave your wallet laying on your bed, couldn't you?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes, you bet. You'd leave your wallet, lay right there and be fine with most of them. Once in a while it'd be one that wasn't to be trusted. But very, very few.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't know whether it would be of interest or not to tell him about when you cashed a bunch of Lumberjacks checks for them. Well, that was foolish on my part.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But I was trying to be good too, you know, and some of those fellows were staying in camp. I was going out, well, they wanted me to cash a check for them. So they'd have, you know, place a little poker and stuff. So I had taken some checks with me one day. I had about $400 worth of checks. And as I was going back to camp, I decided I'd fish a little before I got to camp. Fishing was real good over there. And by God, when I got into camp, I reached for that billfold and it wasn't in my pocket. I'd lost it. Fishing.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Gee, well, you never seen a man more than $400 in there. Their money. More like $4 ,000 now or more, you know.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Boy, oh boy. He said he knew. I just throw my fishing, fishing, rigging down and I went right back out in the creek and I walked back up that creek just exactly where it come down, you know. Winding around and looking, looking, looking. And I went under a trestle and as I went under this trestle, I remembered sitting up there on top and changing the hook or baiting the hook or doing something. Well, I better get up there and look. And I went up there and look. I'd sat on a log there and there was another log right behind. There was my billfold right in between them two logs. With these other people. Woo! Boy, was I glad. But if I'd taught me a lesson, I never did it again. I never got no more checks. That sounds like about the worst situation I can imagine. Sounds like a nightmare. I could see the same thing happening to me. Boy, I'd be scared. Oh, you're scared. Because you'd have to replace it even if you had to borrow money. I'd have to replace that money because ain't nobody would have believed that I lost it, see. Well, it was my responsibility to bring it back. And I would have had to replace that. And I'm sure I'd had to work all summer to make that much money. Because I was, I don't know, I was scaling that time, I think. And I don't know, I was getting about $80 a month or something like that. That would have been one hell of a job to repay that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find that guys had a hard time accepting your scale?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I've heard from scalers that, you know, there were, you know, some fellas just would never, couldn't believe that, you know, whatever it was, it wasn't enough. Well, I don't believe that's true. I scaled for many years and I can't ever remember having any trouble in that line. Some scalers did. But I think if you tried to be fair and tried to do the right thing, that you didn't have no worries. You had no problem at all.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And that's all Potlatch ever asked you to do, wasn't it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You bet. Potlatch never asked me to cheat for them in no way.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And I didn't. And I tried to give the sawyers or skidders or whatever, I was getting bored just exactly what I thought was fair. And I never had any trouble at all. No problem. I think that's the only way you can do any worse, do as near right as you know how.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about gyppo?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: When you were in the woods then was mostly gyppo instead of...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Day work?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah. Well, both kinds. They gyppoed for a while and then they decided they'd try to saw logs by the day and they tried that and then they went to gyppo again. I don't know.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Was there a combination of gyppo and day in the same camp at the same time?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That I don't quite remember. I don't know. Well, certain people, the ones that were doing the...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, I imagine you were on a salary for your scaling, right?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Salary, yeah. So there were certain positions like that that were probably just only salary. Oh, yeah. And then these others were... Well, I sawed logs in camp before I ever went into scaling or anything like that and that was gyppo. I was there for... Wynton Lumber Company up on...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the name of that? Name of that creek up there? Fernwood? Is that a pardon?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Ah, where all of them... Where they mined them, garnets.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do they call it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Emerald...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Emerald Creek?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Emerald, Emerald Creek. No, I sawed logs up there and that was gyppo and we had to take the scale there. That's the scaler's kiddos. Well, you know, some men had told me that one thing they didn't like about the gyppo was that the more work you done, the more likely there was to cut down the rate. That's true, that's true, that's true. Big companies, they're watching that.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
