words: If you made too much, well, they'd be trying some way to cut the price down on you.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: On an individual basis?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You mean on a... Well, they'd pretty much run the whole camp.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Some of the things didn't seem quite fair because when they worked with the day and you thought that production was getting down a little bit, why, then you... The thing to do is to fire the saw gang that was on the lower end of the totem pole and that would spruce up everybody else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The lower end of the totem pole meaning that what guys were producing last?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: At least. That would shake the others up to make them work a little faster. That would shake up the others and they'd produce more for a long day work. Of course, the gyppo done away with that, see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How much direction did you get as a camp foreman?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, how much were you... Did you get, you know...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The wages? No, how much did you get told what to do? How much direction did you get? How much were you directed?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Not... They didn't come out and throw their voice much as to what you had to do.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, there had to be some influence. I can't quite remember how that went about.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Who was directly above you, Bradbury?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Bradbury and Hornby. Bradbury was the superintendent.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And then Hornsby was what?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He was a walker. He was kind of over the trains and that sort of thing.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't remember him being around an awful lot, either one of them.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Not very much, no. No, they left it alone pretty well. Probably if things went well.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How long were you foreman?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, from the time McKinnon got his heart attack. Not really very long. Until we left there, we left there the second of February.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Of 43?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Next summer. And then we stayed on. When the snow got so deep we couldn't hardly produce anything. Seven feet of snow on the level.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That was a pretty good reason to leave it, wasn't it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, I don't know what you can do with that kind of snow anyway. Seven feet on the level.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: They'd have to shovel it out to get down because you had to make what?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Set 14 inch stumps. So they'd have to shovel down 14 inch stumps. A lot of work to get to catch you. Damn right. You had to have a snow shovel with every gang of saws. Shovel down. And then, my God, you're down in that hole there and from that tree you went to go, you had to have a passage out of there. Terrible.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you think about, did you mind giving up school teaching?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Not for Mary. Not for Mary. She married me. She was still a school teacher.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you continue to teach after you got married?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No. No, I didn't teach at all after we were married. Oh, after we were married. No, I was teaching second grade at Kellogg and I just told my superintendent I wanted to get married and at that time, well, it would have been too hard, it was too far to try to commute or anything and so he found somebody to replace me. And then we lived in camp for several months and then we came out and then we've been here ever since. We've been here ever since.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh really?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: In our home ever since.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: We spent a year out on the coast. During the war. During the war. Worked for Boeing. Worked for Boeing.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But other than that, we have been right here all the time. 34 years this November.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yep.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you ever consider teaching again?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I have taught. I have taught since then. Yes, I have taught. I taught in Deary and Bovill. I have taught, I taught until the year before last.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh, really?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I taught first grade in Deary, Bovill for... You know, her family pretty well raised when she taught again. For several years. I had the idea in lots of places that women couldn't teach once they were married. They wouldn't even let them. That was back, yes. That was about that. Well, they were changing right about the time that I married Oscar because so many of the women were working in war work at that time. But up until that time, the first few contracts I signed said that if you married, you lost your job. That was right in the contract. Yeah. Won't be done. Uh -huh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have much an idea why they had that kind of clause or what they were trying to do?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I think they felt that a man should support... At that time, no women's led. They felt that a man should support his wife. That a wife should not work. Then they felt that girl, that maiden lady who didn't have any means of support, deserved the job. I'm sure that was the feeling. And I also think that it was... They felt a woman who has a house to care for will be thinking more about her husband and her house and her family than about the school children. That's good to add. I think there was those two...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that was a well -founded kind of idea?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, I do not. I have taught since... I had a husband, a house, and children. And I'm sure that I was a better teacher then than when... Well, of course, mostly because of experience and more training. But I don't think it was a valid reason at all. No, it didn't. But they had those. It doesn't seem fair to me. But at that time, they just... I'm sure they felt that a married woman should not work. I mean, it was just one part of the morality at the time, that a married woman just doesn't work. Her husband was supposed to support her and that the job should be saved.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I've also had the idea that from talking to people that the only jobs that were open to women pretty much was teaching and nursing. And nursing, right.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And that too seems quite different now.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, it's a different world. It truly is.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, she found that I couldn't support her and so she tried to teach me things and she never had no luck there either. So she's had her work cut out for her.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, what have I taught? Three or four different stints, haven't I?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't remember. We had three boys. Let's see, I taught before I was married. And then we had three boys. And when our youngest one, when David was four, I went back to teaching. And I taught a couple of years and then we had a little girl. And then I stayed home until she was school age. And then I taught again. And then that time both Carla and I got sick. And then I stayed home for several years. And then I went back and taught part time for a while. And then I taught the last, and I taught for about eight years. And then my mother died and my dad lives in, is old and lives in Lewiston. And I stopped teaching a year before I left so that I could be with him. But now we have a housekeeper for him. So now I'm just lonely.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That's good.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I was going to ask you on the whole, if you noticed much difference between the lumber operations up here, the potlatch lumber company and the clear water lumber company down there, was there any noticeable difference?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, I wouldn't say there's too much difference. It's pretty much. Because it was all potlatch farms. It was all the same company and owners. I can't see much difference. I've heard they had better horses up here when they were using horses.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there's truth to that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I don't know. Their horses were tops. My goodness. Well, I believe maybe that's true because Naugle was pretty much of a horse lover. And I do think he had about the cream of the crop when it comes to picking out horses. But I was a little better acquainted here at that time with the horses. And I was almost fair water. So I don't know.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: What year did they phase out the horses as a rule?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, it was kind of gradual, but it must have started about

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: probably at 35, 36. It sounds about like what I heard. And then they were gone by 40, I think. Very few left for 40.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Because when I went in there in 42, there weren't any horses, although they had a barn boss.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And one horse. All out the garbage with one horse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, yeah. I only remember the ones. All I ever saw.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But still Earl Thorpe was barn boss.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I remember wondering, barn boss with one horse?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Getting pretty busy.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened in the Depression when the Depression hit? Did you get laid off? In 32?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah. I hadn't worked in the woods a great lot before that. Sawed logs and worked on the section and stuff like that. And boy, she was tight in the Depression. You couldn't get a job out of no kind.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Even those that were working in the woods where they laid off? Even those that were working? What'd you do?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, I worked on the well drill and tried a little farming.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Shot game out of season.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Had to eat? Do you think that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, I tell you, that was pretty rough.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: The statue of limitations is out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think it's run out that they've been special?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I think they'd have to arrest about half the county. Everybody outside of Moscow. Probably some people in there, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I can't imagine, though, were people worried about getting caught doing that kind of thing?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: It seemed like everybody did it. Not too much. Oh, yes. You said you were worried. I didn't like to hit the jailhouse, but I knew I was going to have something to eat.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And he said that a neighbor lady told him that she dreamed that she saw him kill out of season. She had seen him, and she was just trying to tell him. She was trying to get it out of me, get me to admit to it. She said she dreamed that she saw him kill, game out of season.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, it was her boy that come and told me that, that hurt him. That his mother had had a dream. Oh, I didn't remember that story. He smiled. He thought he was doing so smart. He didn't know how close he'd come to getting a fist right in my mouth. But I knew that was no good either. I would have ended up in jail if I'd have done that. So I didn't. But he didn't. He didn't baffle him way too much, because I don't bluff very easily. But you were guilty. I was scared. I don't think I showed it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How many other guys have you met?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: There was a lot of guys around here that did it. I've heard them called, I've heard a deer called Governor Cattle.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, when they actually did need it to feed their families, you cannot fault them too much.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, not at all. Oh, we didn't waste any. We didn't waste any of it.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, except in the fact that if you killed them out of season, there may not be any then for the next people.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, how long did it go on before you wound up getting on solid ground again?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, 30, I can't remember when it was that... You said you were working in the camps in 36. Oh, yes, I've been in the camp before that.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, I don't know, about 34, I guess. Maybe 35. No, 34, I think. Did you ever get in one of the CCC camps or that sort of thing during that... I worked in the blister rust, yes. Yes, I worked in the blister rust one year. I don't know, that was probably in 33 probably.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did they do on that blister rust? How did they attack that blister rust? Was it all the ribes they were trying to pull? How would they go about doing that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, you had to go out there and you'd run a string through there, so you worked between these two strings and then you pulled these bushes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By hand? They come out just pulling them?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, you had a Pulaski there, if they didn't come easy, well, you helped them out with that. But that was just a mag work deal, it didn't help as far as blister rust was concerned, I don't think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think they thought that it would?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, yes, yes. That was what they told us anyway.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And what was it, wasn't it barberry and gooseberry?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Gooseberry.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And then there wasn't something else though besides gooseberry, was there?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: There were three or four different kinds of bushes. All ribes, all that kind. They were all ribes, yeah. They were supposed to... The blister rust was supposed to... Host those spores.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were there many young men from the cities that were working on that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The same time you were?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You're darn right, you bet.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But they were more in the three C camps, weren't they, than in the blister rust? Or were they?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I don't know, maybe.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I was never in the CCs. We were strictly blister rust. Oh, boy, I'll never forget going to that blister rust that time. Been laying around home, you know, no work, hadn't been for quite a while, soft, went up and got this job, and boy, that was wonderful.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I think, I don't know what, they paid $80 a month or something like that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Boy, that was going to be real good. Went up to Clarkia and this fellow took us out to the pickup over to a trail and put us on this trail and he said, just follow this and you'll get to the camp. And he says, it's nine miles. It's nine miles across. And I believe it was nine mile by section line because I never seen nine miles so long in my life. Had a pack sack with our rig on, you don't have to carry that. You were ashy. Oh, yes, and I'll have to tell him.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Remember, did you ever hear of Frank Tom, the old Chinaman up at Clarkia?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He had a store in there. And when I went to work up there in the blister rust, I didn't have only a pair of oxfords, you know, or smooth shoes and was going to go back in the woods. And I needed a pair of shoes. And believe you me, I didn't have the price of the pair of shoes, which was probably about $10 then. And I went into old Frank Tom and I told him I was going to go to work in the blister rust and I needed a pair of shoes. And he had never seen me before. I had never seen him before either. And he didn't even ask me my name.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He says, what size do you wear?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And so I told him what size and he got me a pair of shoes and I put them on and went to camp. And I never paid him a penny down and he didn't even ask me my name. And believe you me, he was the first man I visited when I got my first check. That was a good turn. And boy, I'll never forget old Frank Tom for that. Because I don't know, I didn't have a dime when I went up there to go to work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think, do you think that he decided to trust you just because of the way you talked to him?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't know. I don't know. I have no way of knowing because he didn't even ask me my name or didn't have to sign nothing or anything.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he there for a long time at Clark?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Long, long time, you bet. Many, many years.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: In fact, I think he was there after we were married. It could be.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: It seems as though that he surely - Or was it his daughter that was there or something?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't know. I remember going into that old store and you telling me this story. And I can't remember if that old fellow was there or not.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But that was his store. He ran, it was his store, general store there. General store there. I remember. It's a burn. No, it wasn't there anymore. And speaking about stores, before I asked you that, you then said that then you had to hike in. And that was like, you were way out of shape. Were you out of shape when you started working in the - that sounded like rough work to me, pulling bushes all the time. Oh, no, that wasn't there at all. It wasn't too bad. Most of the walking that you found after you were out of shape. Yeah. You just wandered along through the bushes looking for those things. And then you had a ball of string, you know, you strung that parallel.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Did you get room and board in $80, do you think?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: My God, I couldn't tell you that.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And what year was that, do you think?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Probably 33 or 34. 33 or 34, 33 probably. Well, really, that was good wages compared - the first year I talked was 40. Oh, it was like buying and falling in a gold mine, you bet. And I only got $80 a month. And I had to pay for my room - I had to pay $20 for room and board. Teaching, yeah. Teaching, yeah. Country school had all eight grades. I think you got that contract here. I think I do. I think I still have the contract.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where did you teach?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I taught in a country school up by Craigmont.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And I made $80 a month and had to walk - I must have walked - You made $80 a month?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: $80 a month. And you paid 20 - And I paid $20 a month for board and room. It was only an eight month school because they couldn't afford to have a nine month school. And I must have walked - You did your own janitor work. I did my own - oh, damn it. I lit my own fire and did my own janitor work. And I must have walked - oh, I don't know. I suppose it was two and a half - I guess it was about two and a half miles from where I stayed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Each way?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Back at the timber. Oh, that's a long way. Plan changed. It changed. The old Hart School at Craigmont for the first year I taught, 1940.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was much expected of you in the community as the teacher, as the school teacher?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Did you have a part to play in the - Oh, I don't quite know. You know, I'm thinking of - I've thought from what some people have told me, that a lot of the social life for the community had centered around that school.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, we did. Yeah, yeah, we did. We had community dances there, and I really wasn't - I don't think I was obligated to go, but I lived with the people in the community, and so I went. You know, but I didn't organize the dances myself. But we had big potluck dinners there, and I did help organize some of them for the whole community. And it was a community center, because it was many miles out of Craigmont. I suppose we were probably - I think we were about eight miles out of Craigmont, straight north of Craigmont. And that was the community center, was the school. And it - And we used to have literary programs, and I did help with those. We'd get people in the community to put on plays.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Debates?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, plays. No, we never did debates. We never did debates, no.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you have that when you were in school, the debates?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I can remember - But I went to school up there during the whole time.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you find it really enjoyable to teach? Did you, when you were first starting, or did you find it a little overwhelming with all the kids?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I had gone to a country school, and I think that helps a lot. Because I can just imagine - I met many girls that I taught with, that I went to school with, that then went out to teach in country schools. If they hadn't also grown up in that type of a community, and had also seen how other teachers taught all these grades, they were overwhelmed. But I do think that the ones that had that experience in their childhood weren't as quiet as panicky.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: What do you think it was that it - Eight grades.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Boy. Then they panicked. Because, you know - There isn't panic.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't see how it's possible for one person to teach eight grades.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But now, I take it back. There could have been eight grades, but there weren't that first year. But then I taught over here - I can't quite remember, there weren't all eight grades anyway, and there weren't that many children.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But do you know where Mount Tomer is?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You know where the schoolhouse used to be?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: More or less. Oh.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You know where Roger Smith lives?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, I don't think I know his - Have you been out on that road?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Not really, but I had a rough idea about where it was. I taught at Mount Tomer. And that year, I had at least one child in every grade. I had - And I don't want to say too many, but I think it was seven first graders. Either five or seven, and I believe it was seven first graders. And, yeah, at least one - I believe there were 27 kids in that school. And that was hard. And that was hard. Oh, well I guess. You know, with that many that had never been to school before. Eight grades, gee whiz! The Ingerbritsen kids were there that year.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Fritz Flomer, do you know Fritz Flomer?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: No, but I know the name. Uh huh. Her dad's name. Yeah.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I taught the - I taught - Was there more than one Flomer?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I remember Fritz especially. I see his name in the paper. I haven't seen Fritz since that - since I taught him the other day. But I see his name in the paper.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: The thing you know, you'll be telling this man how old you are, Mom. Getting way back in ancient history.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Wouldn't that be terrible? But I thought maybe you - Have you lived at Moscow for some time?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, I live on the Bear Ridge. I'm not too far from here. Oh, oh, I thought maybe you lived at Moscow, so I was going to tell you Moscow names. But if you don't - No, I don't actually live as well as people around here. I see. Mm hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But that would have been in what now?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: 41 maybe?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: God, I think so. It would have been a big change, even in our lifetime.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I was so surprised to hear that woman here, Mrs. Bowe, here last week say that she taught in a country school in South Dakota. And I didn't even realize there were any country schools left. Because there hasn't been in Idaho since about the time that I taught back in the early 40s. Yeah, that's right. She said she was teaching in a country school. She was teaching in a country school this last year. In South Dakota. In South Dakota. And that truly amazed me.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Good for me.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you, after you got out of school, did you do much in Deary? Did you have any connection with the town? I mean, ride along? Did you always trade there mostly?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, oh yes, that was our town.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And he played for dances in Bovill and Deary and around. Oh, yes, yes, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did you play?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Saxophone. Oh, yeah, we had dances. That helped put us through the Depression.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: People kept dancing during the Depression?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, there would be a few come.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where at would you?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I played here in Deary and played in Bovill. But not even where in Deary at the old... Oh, up above the Mercantile.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Did you charge a certain amount or did they just pitch in?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, they charged.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Probably 25 cents?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't remember. Then we played out in the old opera building in Bovill. It's still standing, you know. Boy, that was a rickety thing. Gee, we played shot issues in it. Flooring up and down like that, people sitting along the side, you know, he had to be golden.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Was it because the building was that old at that time?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, it was pretty dilapidated. It never was very strong, I don't think. In Deary, most people that came to the dances, just townspeople were people coming from the countryside.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I suppose the biggest part was right from town. Few outsiders, all right. Oh, we was getting quite a rap built up. Pretty good attendance, considering the times and everything.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I think in the Depression that people didn't go to more dances.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Why?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: More communities. Communities minded. They didn't set home and watch television.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Probably didn't have the money to go traveling. I was going to say, they couldn't go very far to go to a dance, so they had to patronize their own community. They had to do something.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, you know, Deary doesn't seem like it has much going on in it anymore. In the way of business and all that. I'm wondering when that decline set in, when it kind of fell off there. Because it just seems like when you look at the old magazines from the school and the old newspapers, you see all these stores and businesses, and they're all gone now.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I really don't know if it ever had any more population than it does now, though, did it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, I think so.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Did it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, well, I'm just wrong. I'm sure.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But haven't we got a picture of all the school kids?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Marching to some day, marching there. And my God, it must be 100 kids going to school there.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Have we got one of those pictures?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't think so. I don't think you have it. I really don't have it. But it's the businesses that seem to be gone that used to be...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And there was a bank?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, hell yes. It was quite a town at one time. Pretty darn good. The bank and post office and confectionaries and bakery and harness shop and a couple of garages and creamery station and all good. It was just a pretty fair town. Depot and...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: What do you think the population was at the height? Do you have any idea?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, I wouldn't. I just wouldn't.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you have much of an idea about when it started to go downhill?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I believe about the time when the Depot was discontinued. I wonder when that was. After the Second World War or... Oh yeah, it must have been. I don't know if that's it, but it seems like it never was...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there a Depot agent here?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was the last one? Do you know?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't even know. I think Mrs. Jim Baker was the last one. Oh.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I'm just trying to remember what it was like when the Depot was up there.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: She can't remember. Oh, one thing I was going to ask you, Ballot, did you know that...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't Chuck, Chuck Wills' wife that then after she left him went up to and ran the store at Helmer?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, she married Dan Rolts. That's right.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you know, did you know them at all? Were they there for a very long time at Helmer?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, I can't, I just can't remember how long they were there. I remember both of them real well.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And then he married another one, another colored person.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was her name?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I'm not sure I knew. I'm not sure.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened to his first wife?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Did she die or did they...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: The wife? Did she die or did they split up? The second one?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: The first one. Chuck's ex -wife. The second one, you mean. The first one, you know, they split up. Oh, then Chuck married again. Yeah. That's what you're talking about. Oh,

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: she, they split up too. And she lived with this Hanson, Art Hanson and all.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I didn't really know.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, Art Hanson, he still lived them though. Okay, that was Chuck's second. Mm -hmm. Second wife. Do you think, you think people, I'm a little surprised, it sounds like a, not the kind of thing that would go on much in those days. I mean, the racially mixed marriages, nowadays it's pretty acceptable, but back then it seems like people were, must have been more... People kind of looked down on it a little bit, but no one made any big issue of it, as I remember.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Probably a few jokes went along with it, I don't know.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I guess they called it the black and white store. Yeah, they called it the black and white store. That's not very derogatory. No, not really.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't think it was very acceptable to Dan's children at all.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Why he'd been married before then. Yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: By the way, did you ever get the booklet that we put out on Arthur Burke?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes, we read that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was there more than one?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, for maiden women.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And if they didn't have a man to support them, then that was about, they had to either teach or nurse, that was about the only thing that was... No, not Arthur. Rather than the fact, you know, rather than the fact that teachers didn't marry, I think many, many of them did. What most of them would do would go out into the community to teach, and then teach their two years, and then marry one of the young farmers, one of the young men that lived in that community. I think that happened really many, many more times than the ones that didn't marry. But the reason you saw so many old maiden school teachers, I think, is because that was about the only thing that they could do to make a living. Either that, or work as a hired woman, or as a hired girl. Which wouldn't be as good work for them, probably.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, I kind of, I wondered that maybe, maybe that might have influenced them to not...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I mean, it seems like there was, you almost had to make a choice there, between a career and a family. Yes, uh -huh. Now let's see, in that first school that I taught, the girl that taught there before I did, had married a local boy, local young farmer, and then they wouldn't let her teach the next year. They had the, it was just understood that if you got married, you just didn't teach. She had her job cut out for her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that, do you think then that, that some, some women might have, who were teachers, simply might have made the choice that they would prefer to have a career than to, than marriage?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I mean, I was thinking perhaps some of them would have married if they, if they could have combined the two.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's possible?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't know, but it's...

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Of course, of course, even as far back, even as, as recently back as the early 40s, it was almost a stigma not to be married, you know, whereas now they have a choice, you know. But, you know, that was just the only thing they did, was you just got married, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think there was much pressure on, on single, on single women to marry?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I know there was.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I know there was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What form do you think it would take?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, just nearly everybody.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, you're not married yet?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I was 21. I wasn't, I was 21 when I was married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And still people, you mean you're not married yet? You know, you mean you're not married yet?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: My goodness, as if you were the worst old man you've ever...

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's the matter with you?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, yeah. A good looking girl like you should be married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's wrong with you?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You mean you're not, oh, definitely there was pressure. Would they, do you think, do you think that, that with a single, a single woman would be likely to have people trying to fix her up with... Oh, no, no doubt about it, no doubt about it. Because to most people you just, unless you were some kind of a freak or, you know, you just, that was just the traditional thing that you do. Well, still is, marriage is still acceptable, but I mean it isn't quite as, as pushed on, on people now. I still think my 21 year old is a little girl. Yes, we'd feel terrible if she even got interested in me. But I, that I, that definitely was a lot of pressure put on people.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You think then that, it's funny, I haven't talked to many, to many single older ladies and I've kind of wondered if there's a way I could, I could ask them about that. I'm not sure, I feel like I'm pretty sensitive. Maybe a woman could do it if she's talking about, I'm not sure that I can.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: To ask about that. But I do, I'm, you know, definitely there was, you just, there was an awful lot of pressure.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Do you, do you think that, I mean, do you have much idea, do you think it'd be, did some people just wouldn't, I mean, I, I've known a bachelor of men, now that seems a little more common to me. I kind of wonder why, you know, in those days that anybody, how anybody managed to resist the pressure to get married, you know. All those lumberjacks did, most of them did anyway. Guy like Charlie Jelber, guy like Arthur Burke, they never did marry.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, both good guys would have been good fathers, both of them. And Arthur. Look at these other bachelors living around. I remember how Arthur liked our kids and he'd come and he'd stand on his head for them. He'd do anything to entertain those kids. And we said at that time he certainly should have had grandchildren because he just, just loved kids.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Gosh, yes, he must have been what, 65 years old and standing on his head here to show all the kids.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who's standing on his head?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, oh, Arthur Burke. Oh yes, he just, and he'd play with our kids. Play with the kids, he's a woman.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you get any idea why a guy like him never, never married? Probably felt responsible for his mother, don't you think?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Could be. He's told me, he told me that he, that he took care of his mother. So I kind of think that he probably felt responsible.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: For her. Well, yeah, and I think the only, only way that would ever have been, would have been for some woman to ask him. I don't think he'd. Still, he wasn't shy. Well, not really, I guess. I don't know. He had quite a reputation for work. Oh, you bet. People told me he worked awful hard. He never could be outdone. He had to beat you a little bit, no matter what he did. I know he was putting up some siding in the bedroom there, on each side of the door, and we had strips about this long, you know, and I always kind of liked to tease him, you know, or tease him, all I could do. I'm pretty good at driving nails myself, you know. And so we was boarding up here, we had the boards all cut, and I was putting them on. Not a word was said. Not a word was said about being a racer or anything like that, you know, but I was going to beat Arthur by a little bit, you know, I was just driving him, and he could see, I guess, that I was trying to beat him or something, and boy, he lit into them nails, you never seen anything like it, and he beat me, of course.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And not a word was said either before or after. And he was an old man when they were building the granary down here, and he would just, Oscar was afraid of hype.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oscar's always been afraid of hype, and old Arthur would just dance around that after they got a nail up so high that you would spin. And we just set a two by four up in the corner with a little short brace on, you know, and then we're going to put a plate across the top there. He had to get up there and sit on top of that post up on top of there. Well, there was no sense to that. He didn't have to get up there. But he thought that was smart to get up there, and then he hollered so Hazel should look out the window to see him. Probably made you feel a little queasy. Well, I'm not good on hype. I can't do it. Well, some people just aren't. I know. I shouldn't be that way because my dad was an old sailor, and it didn't bother him at all to get up on hype. I never seen anything like that. But I couldn't.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Another thing that I thought too, with maybe with some women who didn't want to get married, maybe the men too, is that maybe they had impossible ideals, you know, because I had the idea that in the early days, or in the more or less like the end of the Victorian times, you know, the people idealized, you know, the their perspective made or something, and then if you couldn't find somebody that ever measured up, but then I don't know, they're just ideas that I don't know. Another thing is that maybe they felt that they wasn't capable of making a living for the for an extra person. Because times were pretty tough a lot of the time. I suppose I've contributed, but how much I wouldn't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Would you say that times were tough here before the Depression too, like in the 20s? Or would you say those times were times were rather prosperous?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: A part of the 20s, you know, the just after the First World War that things was fabulous, you know, before the big break. Criminy, I know my dad sold wheat for $2 and a half a bushel back in them days. And that's when $2 and a half was worth a lot of money. But, well, of course, he ended up not getting that too. It was a long time and then times got tough and he couldn't never pay it back.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All on time, what do you mean?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, when it dropped in 29, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But who owed him the money?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: All the neighbors that had bought wheat for seed and stuff like that. Oh, he was owed money then.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Oh, now I know what would be interesting about your dad's actually his thin dime.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: His what?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: His thin dime that you still have. Oh, yes, yes.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: You bet. This was when I was pretty young kid. My dad, they were hard up. I mean, the money was just scarce and he had to go to town to buy something. We needed something real bad for the house. And he had a little dime, a dime that was up in the clock. He does it. The date on it is 1838. The date on this dime. And he had to have something out of town real bad. So he took that dime to go up and buy something in town. And when he got to town. He knew it was an antique bin and he hated to spend it. He hated to spend that old dime. And when he got to town, I don't know, he met somebody that owed him something or he somehow or another he got the groceries without spending that dime. And that dime went back in the clock. And we have it to this day. And I still have it. To the last dime. I thought that was right down to the last dime. We still have it. So, yeah, that's that's getting down pretty close. It really is. You bet. Well, we have a book of records that my mother kept.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't that in 1906?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That their total income for the year was $64. And they had an outlay of 48.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Wasn't it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I can't remember. I can't remember. I'd be real curious to see that if you still have it. I think it's up in that old suitcase out there. I believe it is. I'm not sure. I'm really not. Three of these Irishmen were going to whip him out on the sawdust pile. And he whipped all three of them out of the slither and whistle.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And he said that old Swanson over here, he was another Swede working there. And old Swanson was taking sawdust away or something down there. Swanson was underneath there peeking out from underneath me. He wouldn't come out and get in the fight. He was scared. This means they didn't strike. They didn't strike. Well, no, my dad wasn't supposed to fight or anything like that on the job. Probably all got canned. After the fight, he went up to the office to call for his time. Because that was part of his, you know, and he got in trouble. He had to get the hell out and go somewhere else. When he went up to the office and called for his time, the boss told him, he asked him why, of course, and told him. Well, he said, you're going to stay. And he says, them other guys are going to go. So he stayed and the other guys was canned.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was that around here in the sawmill?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't know. One of those sawmills. You got a picture of it, I think. He said, we're going to have separate bunk houses for this week. He said, we're going to have separate bunk houses for the Swedes and the Irish. And boy, that didn't go over very well. Fighting words. So they were going to clean up on him. But they didn't have any luck.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he a big man, your father?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, he weighed about 185, not a picture you can see. Yeah, yeah. Not a tall man, though. Not a tall man. Not tall, no. I was taller than he was. But he weighed about as much as I ever weighed.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was he built about like our ridge?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Pretty much. About 185 pounds. But he had hands on him that you couldn't believe. He could grip and he was quick as lightning. He was quick. I know a guy down at the saloon come at him with a knife one time. And he got close enough so he could reach him. He hit him and the knife sailed clear of the ceiling. It was all the worst of that. He was strong. I tell him about when the Thracian crew was here. I seen this. I know this happened right out here on the lawn. I don't know how old he was, but he must have been up around 70 years old. Old man. Oscar was 70 days before yesterday.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Old man, wasn't he?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Old man. But anyway, you know how they do in the evening. They twist down on the broom hammer. You know how they, you've seen them do that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What's that?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: To see who could turn the other fellow. And then bring it down and try to twist the other fellow and make him let go. And those other guys had been doing that until one guy was the winner. You know, he was the best one. My dad was sitting there, an old fellow walking with a cane. He says, I said, let me try that. He got up there and he took a hold of that broom handle and he fetched it down like that. That guy told me afterwards he never felt anything like that in his life. He said if he hadn't let go, he said he'd have took the hide right out of his hands. My dad just took that down just like that. It's not like he was not a man to be trifled with. He was strong now, there's no question about it. Arthur Burke tells about that he was the only man he ever saw that could take a wheat sack by the two years like that, full wheat sack, and throw it up over his head like that all the way back and walk off with it. The only man he ever saw that had that much grip in his hand. When you stop to think about it, that takes some doing.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you get along well with your father?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes, yes. I feel sorry now about some of the things I did with my parents, but I think everybody does. You better get along with him, I tell you. That's what I'm thinking. He had a heart as big as all of us. You said he was rough with everyone else except for these kids, you said. Yes, sir. You said that he pampered and babied you kids. He wouldn't take nothing off of anybody, nobody better cross his path, except us kids. And he was so tolerant with us that he put up with murder. He spoiled us from the word go, no question about it. I got by with things that I should not have got by with, and so did my sister.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Like what?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Well, sleeping in and him out here working, and you know, stuff like that. And all he had a heart as big as all outdoors for the family, but not for anybody else. He was straight, he was a straight shooter. By God, he expected the other fellow to be that way, too.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I can't help but think that the reason he was that way is because he had it so tough when he was a kid.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: He tells about, well, he was only eight years old when he had to go out and herd cattle for this big landowner over in Sweden. And didn't have no shoes, and cold in the fall, frosty, and when a cow would urinate, he'd run up and stick his feet in it to warm his feet.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Pretty rough for an eight -year -old, isn't it?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, and working for somebody else besides your own family, too.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That might have been one of the reasons why he was so easy on us, that he didn't want us to have it like he had it. Well, in my opinion, if you were going to be one or the other, it'd be a lot better to be nice to your family and mean to the outside world than to be nice to the outside world and mean to the family. He was mean to others, but he was awful strict with the other fella. He better be on the up -and -up, because that's what he expected.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And I remember him telling about herding these darn cows, and then there was one day it was warm and sunshine, he was up on the hillside, he was only eight years old, and laid down there and went to sleep. And of course the darn cows had to go down to get in this fellow's oak field or something. The old fella seen that, and he'd come up there, and to wake my dad, he just kicked him. Just gave him a hell of a kick.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I know that's set in my dad's memory all the time. He said if he ever went back to Sweden, he was going to kill him. By the time he got big enough that he could do that, well that old fella would probably sit in an old rocking chair somewhere.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was the deal on that, that most of the families, like his family, just kind of worked for this guy?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yes, it was just slaves, I guess. A multitude. He met, they had to ask permission to go and gather up some cute twigs up and brush from wood. I can't imagine that that, I can understand where that would make a man feel that everybody better deal with him as an equal.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: And he left home when he was 13 years old, and he'd never seen his folks after that. But his, but his sister, it's his sister on that picture.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: His sister came out here then, came to, not only to the United States, but right out here and lived in Deary. So he did see his sister after.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you suppose he was the oldest, your dad?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I don't know. Probably. I don't know whether she was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I said he, do you suppose he was the oldest?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: I kind of think so. He was probably the oldest, because he had to go to sea and so on.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that he had a real dislike of aristocrats, or that kind of system, a feudal system?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Indeed, indeed. He never liked to see anyone take advantage of the other fellow.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: But that's, that was memories that stuck in my dad all his life. Remembering that herd and that cow, and I'm getting kicked. So he had a big heart for kids, especially us kids. Spoiled as rotten.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you ever get in any, any fights that you remember yourself?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, I remember he wanted to get in a fight when he was about 70 years old. You, you say you. I'm talking about you. But I've been in a fight. He says did you ever. Oh yeah, I've been in one or two. Not in the mountain. I was always sissy.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Your dad raised you a sissy, huh?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, he raised me a sissy. Don't strike me as a guy that would turn the other cheek. I left some marks on him.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Now he met your mother when she was cooking in one of these saw camps, one of these sawmill camps, didn't he?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, she was cooking for a sawmill over here. When my dad met her.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: A local girl?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: No, no, she come out from Iowa to visit her sister that was out here, and liked it out here, and she and my dad was married.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: That was about 19, what, must have been 1903.

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Do you imagine?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Imagine. When you say this about dealing fair, that he expected other deal fair with him, I'm just wondering more as an example of what that kind of thing would be.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I mean, are you thinking like business deals or exchanging work and that kind of a thing?

speaker: Oscar Olson or Hazel Olson
words: Yeah, well, your word had to be good.
